message:
chore(beads): review-pass-11 — 3 HIGH release-day / maturity / CI-triage beads

Baseline recap: cc-pandas's earlier CRITICAL (d6xa) + zjme + boyr +
1zzp umbrella all CLOSED during the session via parallel-agent work.
Remaining open review beads from prior passes are still in play.

  frankenpandas-60du [HIGH] docs: CHANGELOG.md 671 commits / 12 days
                            behind HEAD. Stats table (788 pub fns,
                            55+ conformance packets) under-reports
                            reality by 2-9x per row. br-4clx release
                            workflow will use CHANGELOG as release
                            notes input — tagging v0.1.0 today
                            would ship dramatically wrong numbers.
                            Proposes one-shot catch-up commit
                            (conventional-commit-prefix-driven)
                            plus git-cliff / release-plz automation
                            in CI.

  frankenpandas-7gd4 [HIGH] observability: ZERO tracing / log /
                            #[instrument] integration across all 12
                            crates. No tracing crate in deps.
                            README markets "Profile-proven
                            performance" but users can't introspect
                            "where did time go?" without wrapping
                            every call in Instant::now(). br-diic's
                            PyO3 binding needs Rust-side spans to
                            bridge into Python logging / cProfile
                            via pyo3-tracing. Proposes optional
                            tracing feature per crate + #[instrument]
                            on ~50 high-traffic pub fns + example +
                            CONTRIBUTING.md integration.

  frankenpandas-ffhs [HIGH] ci: monolithic 'checks' job lumps fmt /
                            lint / test / conformance / gates. Each
                            failure appears as a single red badge;
                            reviewers must expand 15-step log to
                            find the failing step. Retry cost: a
                            fmt-only fix reruns all 15 steps (5-8
                            min). Industry pattern: split into fmt
                            / lint / test / conformance / gates
                            parallel jobs with needs: dependencies.
                            Drops PR wall-clock from 7 min serial
                            to ~4 min parallel + makes br-0l5r
                            (cross-platform) and br-kmbc (feature
                            matrix) natural additions rather than
                            breaking the mega-job.

Interlock with prior passes:
- 60du (CHANGELOG) + 4clx (release-plz workflow) — 4clx automates,
  60du catches up.
- 7gd4 (tracing) + diic (PyO3) + ing6 (perf gate) + xha7
  (differential bench) — four-way observability stack. Spans at
  the library level, criterion benches reading those spans,
  PyO3 bridging to Python logging.
- ffhs (CI splitting) + 0l5r (cross-platform matrix) + kmbc
  (feature matrix) + 0a83 (llvm-cov dead step) — CI refactor
  integration point. All four beads converge on the same
  ci.yml rewrite.

Running session totals after 11 review passes:
  2 CRITICAL (d6xa closed)
  37 HIGH (7 closed via swarm; 30 open)
  5 MEDIUM
  1 LOW
= 45 review beads total, 8 already shipped by parallel agents
  (d6xa, zjme, boyr, 1zzp/1zzp.*, partial fd90, excel default
  round-trip, etc.).

Zero shipped-code bugs across all 11 passes. Workspace stays
3171/0/13 with 0 clippy warnings. Swarm velocity is processing
review beads faster than cc-pandas is filing them — filing rate
has clearly hit diminishing returns.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

+{"id":"frankenpandas-60du","title":"[HIGH] docs: CHANGELOG.md 671 commits / 12 days behind HEAD; stats table understates state by ~2-9x per row","description":"CHANGELOG.md maintenance audit.

**Observation.** `CHANGELOG.md` is 536 lines / ~48 KB. Last modified by `git log -1 -- CHANGELOG.md`: **2026-04-10 23:50:20** — "Harden IO column validation".

**Count of commits since last CHANGELOG update:** `git log --oneline <last-changelog-sha>..HEAD | wc -l` → **671 commits**.

**Stats table at the end of CHANGELOG.md (lines 527-536) is also wildly stale:**
  Total commits | 269                       ← reality: 269 + 671 = ~940
  Date range    | 2026-02-13 to 2026-03-17  ← reality extends to 2026-04-22 (today)
  Tags / releases | None (pre-release)       ← still true, but closer to 0.1.0
  Workspace crates | 12                     ← still correct
  Lines of Rust | 84,000+                    ← reality likely 120k+ given recent epics
  Public functions | 788                    ← reality: 1530 (verified 2026-04-22 via grep in br-7cfm)
  Conformance packets | 55+                  ← reality: 430 (verified 2026-04-22 in br-n609)

**Consequence.**
- CHANGELOG.md is the canonical summary a user hitting docs.rs / crates.io / GitHub Releases reads to understand what's in the project. It's 12 days + 671 commits behind reality. Every new user lands on an outdated picture.
- br-4clx (release workflow) will tag v0.1.0 with CHANGELOG.md as release-notes input. If we tag today, the notes say "55+ conformance packets" while reality is 430 — ~9x understatement.
- The CHANGELOG format (features / fixes / chore grouped by conventional-commit prefix) could be auto-generated by release-plz or git-cliff, but is currently hand-maintained. Nobody will keep up with 671-commits-every-12-days cadence by hand; the swarm's velocity outpaces manual documentation.
- br-4clx proposes release-plz for the publish workflow. release-plz IS the CHANGELOG automation — same tool. Landing 4clx closes this gap as a side-effect, but 4clx is itself blocked on br-1d9y (nightly pin), br-36qc (cargo-audit), br-0l5r (cross-platform CI). Meanwhile CHANGELOG keeps drifting.

**Severity.** HIGH — user-facing credibility at release. Also: a CHANGELOG that's stale for 12 days + 671 commits on an active swarm is effectively abandoned documentation, regardless of whether a release ships.

**Fix shape:**

1. Short-term (ship today or tomorrow): one catch-up commit. Use `git log --pretty='format:- %s'` between the last CHANGELOG sha and HEAD, filter by conventional-commit prefix (feat / fix / test / chore / docs), append to CHANGELOG under an "Unreleased" section. Regenerate the stats table using:
   - `git log --oneline | wc -l` (total commits)
   - `grep -c "^pub fn" crates/*/src/lib.rs` (public fns — use the script from br-7cfm once it ships, but a one-shot grep works for now)
   - `ls crates/fp-conformance/fixtures/packets/*.json | wc -l` for packet count
   - `find crates -name '*.rs' | xargs wc -l` for LOC

2. Medium-term: adopt `git-cliff` (or release-plz) with a `cliff.toml` that maps conventional-commit prefixes to CHANGELOG sections. Run in CI as a "chore(changelog)" auto-commit on merge to main. Preserves the Unreleased section until `git-cliff --bump --tag v0.1.0` rolls it forward.

3. Link CHANGELOG entries to bead IDs: the project already uses `(br-frankenpandas-XXXX)` suffix on most commit subjects; `git-cliff` can extract those and link to `.beads/issues.jsonl` for traceability.

4. Once CI auto-commits the CHANGELOG, drop the hand-maintained stats table or auto-regenerate it (same script from fix 1).

Blast radius: 1 one-shot catch-up commit (~200-400 lines appended to CHANGELOG.md), +1 cliff.toml (~30 lines), +1 ci.yml job (~15 lines), +1 line in CONTRIBUTING.md (br-6d5s) pointing at the new auto-regeneration flow. Zero source-logic changes.

Prerequisites:
- None urgent for the one-shot catch-up.
- br-4clx (release workflow) for the automated flow — they should ship together.

**Related:**
- br-4clx (release workflow + release-plz) — this bead becomes moot after 4clx lands (release-plz handles changelogs natively). File separately so it can be closed quickly with a one-shot catch-up.
- br-6d5s (CONTRIBUTING.md) — the changelog policy lives here.
- br-n609 (COVERAGE.md auto-generator) + br-zk1j (API matrix) — sibling auto-generated-doc beads. All three replace hand-maintained stats.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T21:16:29.279408814Z","created_by":"cc-pandas","updated_at":"2026-04-22T21:16:29.279408814Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["changelog","docs","release","review"]}

 {"id":"frankenpandas-6d5s","title":"[HIGH] collab: no CODEOWNERS / PR template / CONTRIBUTING.md despite active multi-agent swarm — zero review-routing infrastructure","description":"Contributor / collaboration infrastructure audit.

**Observation.** `.github/` contains exactly one directory (workflows/) and nothing else. Confirmed absences:
  .github/CODEOWNERS                      — no review routing
  .github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE.md        — no PR checklist
  .github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/                 — no issue templates
  /CONTRIBUTING.md                        — no contributor guide
  /CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md                     — no code of conduct
  /SECURITY.md                            — no disclosure (br-8k1i covers separately)

**Context.** This repository is running an ACTIVE multi-agent development swarm — that's not speculation. Evidence from today's git log alone:
- 20+ commits across 6 hours from multiple agents (cc-pandas + Dicklesworthstone + parallel workers).
- br-frankenpandas-iy8u: the original bead was a doctrine note about agents bundling co-landed work with misleading titles. Closed today by c9e9fff ("docs(workflow): require pre-commit diff stat and co-landed bead IDs").
- br-frankenpandas-lxhr (monolith bead): cites rebase conflicts between parallel agents on fp-frame/src/lib.rs as a primary pain point.
- Pre-commit hooks at `.git/hooks/hooks.d/pre-commit/50-agent-mail.py` prove the swarm coordinates through agent-mail infra.

Despite the swarm being active, there is NO documented process for:
- Which agent reviews which area of code (no CODEOWNERS).
- What a PR must include before merge (no PR template).
- How external contributors (humans) onboard (no CONTRIBUTING.md).
- What behavior is expected in issues / PRs (no CODE_OF_CONDUCT).
- How scientific / pandas-behavior-divergence discussions should be classified (no issue template distinguishing bug / feature / conformance-question / docs).

**Consequences already visible:**
- br-iy8u root cause (cod-style commits bundling co-landed work) persists as oral doctrine rather than enforceable CODEOWNERS routing.
- New slice-1 child bead (1zzp.1) had two agents working the same file simultaneously; the CODEOWNERS + PR template would have routed one to review the other's draft rather than both editing in parallel.
- Contributor onboarding is zero-documented: a new agent (this cc-pandas session is literally "fresh cc on new account" per the opening user message) had to reconstruct workflow from context clues.

**Severity.** HIGH — infrastructure that compounds across every PR. Every day without it, the swarm eats overhead re-discovering which file to edit and which agent to block on.

**Fix shape:**

1. `.github/CODEOWNERS`:
   - `/crates/fp-types/ @core-types-owner` (or an agent alias like `@cc-frankenpandas-types`)
   - `/crates/fp-frame/ @frame-domain`
   - `/crates/fp-io/ @io-domain`
   - `/crates/fp-conformance/ @conformance-domain`
   - `/.github/workflows/ @ci-owners`
   - `/.beads/ @scrum-bot` (if there's a bead-curator agent)
   - Auto-routes PR review requests; works with the existing multi-agent workflow.

2. `.github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE.md`:
   - Required: linked bead ID (br-frankenpandas-*)
   - Required: conformance / fuzz / performance impact checkbox
   - Required: `git diff --stat` summary (directly addresses br-iy8u doctrine) — autopopulate via the pre-commit hook already in place
   - Required: which live_oracle_* tests the PR touches / exercises
   - Required: acceptance criteria from the child bead

3. `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/bug_report.md`, `feature_request.md`, `conformance_divergence.md`:
   - Conformance divergences get their own template because they flow to DISCREPANCIES.md, not to a regular bug tracker.

4. `CONTRIBUTING.md`:
   - How to install rch + cargo-fuzz + pandas oracle.
   - How to file a bead (`br create --no-db ...` per today's doctrine).
   - AGENTS.md link for swarm-specific workflow.
   - How to run conformance (`rch exec -- cargo test -p fp-conformance --lib`).
   - How to regenerate fixtures (once br-boyr ships the pipeline).
   - Semver and release expectations (once br-4clx ships).

5. `CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md`: boilerplate Contributor Covenant. Five minutes to add.

Blast radius: 5 new files, no source code changes. Each file is independently useful — can ship as 5 small PRs instead of one.

Related:
- br-frankenpandas-iy8u (workflow doctrine) — already closed via the c9e9fff commit that added pre-commit diff-stat requirement. This bead captures the codepath-level enforcement (CODEOWNERS), the human-facing documentation (CONTRIBUTING), and the PR-time enforcement (template) that the doctrine bead acknowledged were needed.
- br-frankenpandas-8k1i (SECURITY.md) — sibling file in the /github root; coordinate rollout.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T20:31:37.124310007Z","created_by":"cc-pandas","updated_at":"2026-04-22T20:31:37.124310007Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["collab","infrastructure","review","swarm"]}

Implement a dataframe_pivot fixture operation if missing, add strict and duplicate-error packet fixtures against the pandas oracle, wire a packet-filter smoke test, and verify through rch-routed fp-conformance test/check/clippy.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-18T12:54:43.939570216Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-18T13:03:30.908493406Z","closed_at":"2026-04-18T13:03:30.906681527Z","close_reason":"Added DataFrame pivot conformance operation, FP-P2D-128 fixtures, oracle support, and smoke coverage","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"frankenpandas-7g7d","title":"fp-columnar Column.factorize parity","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-21T06:46:53.585301912Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-21T07:06:13.000861521Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T07:06:12.999087372Z","close_reason":"Completed","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
+{"id":"frankenpandas-7gd4","title":"[HIGH] observability: zero tracing / log / #[instrument] integration — users can't introspect 'why is my groupby slow'","description":"Observability / tracing-integration audit.

**Observation.** grep across the entire workspace:
  - `tracing::`                 → zero matches
  - `::info!`, `::warn!`, `::debug!`, `::trace!` → zero matches
  - tracing / log / env_logger / slog in Cargo.toml → zero matches

Zero observability infrastructure. Every fp-* public function runs silently. The ONLY instrumentation is the `EvidenceLedger` type (used inside RuntimePolicy-bound operations) which records alignment decisions at the dataframe-logic level — not a general-purpose tracing / metrics surface.

**Context on why this matters for a pandas-parity library:**
- pandas users have a tradition of interactive tuning — "why is my groupby slow?" is answered by `%%time`, `pd.set_option('display.max_rows', ...)`, etc. The Rust equivalent for integrated profiling is `tracing::span!` + a `tracing-subscriber` layer. We ship neither the spans nor hooks for the subscriber.
- `Profile-proven performance` claim in README: for users to VERIFY the profile, they need introspection into where time goes. Without tracing, they must wrap every call in `Instant::now()` manually.
- br-diic (PyO3 bindings) will expose fp-frame to Python users. Python users DO have mature observability (logging, sys.settrace, cProfile). The Rust backend must emit spans the Python wrapper can bridge to those — see [PyO3 tracing example](https://github.com/PyO3/pyo3/discussions/2270). Without in-Rust spans, the Python profiler sees a single "fp_frame.groupby_sum" call taking 200ms and can't drill down.
- br-bahw (concurrency fuzz) + future rayon integration: concurrent sections need `tracing::instrument` to attach spans to tasks. Without spans, concurrent debugging is impossible.
- br-d6xa / br-qi6y (live oracle CI) — conformance gates produce JSON artifacts. Tracing spans would emit structured events parallel to the JSON artifacts, letting ops correlate "which test ran what" at scale.

**Skill rules / industry norms:**
- Tokio, Axum, sqlx, arrow itself — all integrate `tracing` spans at public entry points.
- Rust API Guidelines don't mandate tracing, but for library-sized crates (more than ~5k LOC) it's expected.
- `#[instrument]` macro is zero-cost when the subscriber discards the span (compile-out via features + proc-macro).

**Severity.** HIGH — library maturity + release-readiness gap. Adding tracing later is expensive (every public fn becomes a touch point); adding it now is mechanical.

**Fix shape:**

1. Add `tracing = { version = "0.1", default-features = false, optional = true }` to [workspace.dependencies].

2. Each fp-* crate adds a `tracing` optional feature:
   ```toml
   [features]
   default = []
   tracing = ["dep:tracing"]
   ```

3. Every pub fn that does non-trivial work gets `#[tracing::instrument(skip(...), fields(rows = frame.index().len(), ...))]`. Priority order:
   - fp-frame::DataFrame::groupby / .sum / .agg_named / .agg_multi
   - fp-frame::Series::rolling / .resample / .expanding
   - fp-io::read_* / write_* (both CSV / JSON / Parquet / Excel / Feather / Arrow IPC / SQL)
   - fp-join::join_series / join_dataframes
   - fp-expr::eval_str / query_str / parse_expr
   - fp-conformance: harness entry points (run_packet_suite, run_e2e_suite) — ALREADY have structured logging via the EvidenceLedger; convert + bridge.

4. Feature-gate the `tracing` dep in downstream crates so users without tracing don't pay the dependency cost.

5. Document in README + CONTRIBUTING.md (br-6d5s): "Enable tracing via `cargo add frankenpandas --features tracing` and attach a `tracing-subscriber`. See examples/trace_my_groupby.rs."

6. Example: `examples/trace_my_groupby.rs` shows the 3-line subscriber setup + sample output.

7. Bridge for PyO3 (once br-diic lands): `pyo3-tracing` translates Rust spans into Python logging records.

Blast radius:
- Cargo.toml updates across 12 crates (+~60 lines total).
- `#[tracing::instrument]` annotations on ~50 high-traffic pub fns (+50-100 lines).
- 1 new example file (~60 lines).
- CONTRIBUTING.md addition (~20 lines).
- Zero source-logic changes. All `tracing::event!(...)` calls compile out when the feature is off.

Prerequisites: None. Can ship incrementally — one crate at a time, one pub fn at a time.

**Related:**
- br-diic (PyO3 bindings) — tracing spans propagate to Python via pyo3-tracing; adding spans now = Python debuggability later.
- br-7cfm (rustdoc + panic contract) — tracing spans at pub fn entry naturally pair with `# Panics:` docs as "when this pub fn is called, here's what you observe + what can go wrong."
- br-bahw (concurrency fuzz) + future rayon — concurrent sections need spans to be debuggable.
- br-ing6 + br-xha7 (perf gates) — tracing spans feed criterion's per-span reporting; same infrastructure as the bench gate.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T21:17:19.821893109Z","created_by":"cc-pandas","updated_at":"2026-04-22T21:17:19.821893109Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["library-maturity","observability","review","tracing"]}

 {"id":"frankenpandas-f9sy","title":"fp-frame Series.at (label scalar access) parity","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-21T10:40:43.707712439Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-21T10:41:12.847708998Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T10:41:12.844937814Z","close_reason":"Already implemented at fp-frame/src/lib.rs:4892 (Series::at(&IndexLabel))","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"frankenpandas-fcck","title":"fp-frame DataFrame.ewm().sum() parity","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-21T10:05:40.874110086Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-21T10:08:25.035953995Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T10:08:25.034089546Z","close_reason":"Implemented DataFrameEwm::sum in commit d5d06b7, 2 tests","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"frankenpandas-fd90","title":"[EPIC] SQL backend abstraction — trait + PostgreSQL + MySQL","description":"Pandas-parity SQL IO across multiple backends. Today fp_io::read_sql / write_sql hardcode rusqlite::Connection; pandas read_sql/to_sql works with any SQLAlchemy-compatible engine (PostgreSQL, MySQL, MariaDB, MS SQL, Oracle, ...). Multi-slice umbrella; file child beads per slice.

1. pub trait SqlConnection in fp-io (DBAPI2-like surface): execute_query(sql, params) -> RowIter, execute_statement(sql, params) -> usize, with_transaction<F>(f) -> Result<T>. Minimum surface to cover rusqlite, tokio-postgres, and mysql_async concrete backends behind the same trait.

2. Cargo features: sql-sqlite (default), sql-postgresql, sql-mysql. Each feature brings its backend crate and a wrapper impl SqlConnection for that backend's Connection type. Additive; disabling sql-sqlite drops the rusqlite dep.

3. read_sql / write_sql / read_sql_table / read_sql_query signatures move from rusqlite::Connection to <C: SqlConnection>. Existing SQLite call sites keep working because rusqlite::Connection gets the wrapper impl.

4. Param binding: closes frankenpandas-tk3k by letting the public surface accept params: &[&(dyn ToSqlBindable)] routed through each backend's type-specific binder.

5. chunksize streaming: read_sql(.., chunksize=Some(n)) returns impl Iterator<Item=Result<DataFrame, ...>> to match pandas.

6. coerce_float: read_sql(.., coerce_float=true) promotes NUMERIC / DECIMAL / MONEY to Float64 at row-materialization time.

7. Live-oracle conformance: packet fixtures per backend (sqlite/postgres/mysql) verifying round-trip parity with pandas' pd.read_sql(engine) for each.

Blocker for m3e8 closure: the README now honestly says 'SQL: SQLite-only via rusqlite; PostgreSQL/MySQL planned' (commit-pending, br-m3e8). Closing that qualifier requires this epic landing — at minimum slices (1)-(3).

Decomposition strategy: (1) is the keystone — pick between (a) a minimal DBAPI2-style trait sufficient to host all three backends without leaking backend-specific types into the public API, vs (b) a typestate / erased-impl approach. (2) feature-gates each backend behind Cargo features to keep binary size bounded. (4)-(7) then unblock incrementally behind (1)+(2)+(3).","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-04-22T12:02:41.189776401Z","created_by":"cc-pandas","updated_at":"2026-04-22T20:04:18.364049452Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["backend-abstraction","epic","io","sql"]}
+{"id":"frankenpandas-ffhs","title":"[HIGH] ci: monolithic 'checks' job lumps fmt/lint/test/conformance/gates — per-PR triage slow + parallelism wasted","description":"CI job-splitting / triage-velocity audit.

**Observation.** `.github/workflows/ci.yml` today has TWO jobs:
  - `checks`: monolithic — runs fmt, cargo check, clippy, workspace test, conformance tests, bench smoke, phase2c gate, governance gate. Each failure surfaces as "checks failed."
  - `fuzz-regression`: new, added recently by br-zjme landing — focused on fuzz corpus regression.

(Note: `checks` also installs Python + pandas as of the br-d6xa fix landing — good.)

**Problem with monolithic checks.**

- Median time for a "checks" run (per recent CI artifacts the ~175s test step + ~60-90s clippy + ~80s install-llvm-cov dead step [br-0a83] + smaller steps) ≈ 5-8 minutes.
- When a single step fails — say, `cargo fmt --check` at step 2 of 15 — every subsequent step is skipped, but the full 5-8 min matrix-slot is consumed because GH Actions doesn't parallelize within a job.
- PR reviewers see ONE red "checks" badge and must expand the logs to find which step failed. If multiple PRs are in flight, triage is serial.
- Retrying a fmt failure re-runs all 15 steps. A minor formatting fix wastes 5-8 min of runner time.
- Caching is per-job. Since `checks` does everything, it sees one cache key for 15 disparate workloads; `clippy` and `test` can't benefit from each other's cache separately.

**How the industry solves it (pattern used by tokio, arrow, polars, serde, etc.):**

```yaml
jobs:
  fmt:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps: [ ..., cargo fmt --check ]

  lint:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps: [ ..., cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings ]

  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps: [ ..., cargo test --workspace --all-targets ]

  conformance:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    needs: [test]   # conformance requires a green test first
    steps: [ install-pandas, cargo test -p fp-conformance --lib ]

  gates:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    needs: [conformance]
    steps: [ ./scripts/phase2c_gate_check.sh, ./scripts/governance_gate_check.sh ]

  fuzz-regression:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest  (already exists)
```

Splits produce:
1. Five PARALLEL jobs (fmt / lint / test / conformance / gates / fuzz-regression) — runner total time drops from ~7 min serial to ~4 min parallel (the longest job is the bound).
2. Per-job status in the PR UI: reviewers see 5 named badges, spot the red one immediately.
3. Retry cost: fmt-only retry reruns fmt only (20-30s instead of 5-8 min).
4. Per-job cache keys: lint + test caches separately; no cross-contamination.

**Severity.** HIGH — impacts every single PR in perpetuity. With the swarm's current velocity (20+ commits/day), 5-8 min of CI serialization per PR adds up to hours-per-week of wall-clock friction that splits would eliminate. Also: the monolithic job blocks future additions (br-0l5r cross-platform matrix, br-kmbc feature-matrix) because adding them to `checks` would push it to ~15 min; separate jobs let each matrix ship independently.

**Fix shape:**

1. ci.yml refactor:
   - Split `checks` into fmt / lint / test / conformance / gates (~50 line reorg, per above).
   - Fuzz-regression job stays as-is.
   - Add `needs:` dependencies so conformance runs AFTER test (no sense running conformance if basic tests fail).

2. Optional enhancement (can ship same PR or follow-up):
   - Add `strategy.matrix` to test job for OS cross-platform (br-0l5r integrates naturally here).
   - Add `strategy.matrix` to test job for feature combos (br-kmbc).

3. Keep llvm-cov install (br-0a83) in its own coverage job or remove the install line until coverage is actually wired.

4. README / CONTRIBUTING (br-6d5s) gets a brief "CI jobs" section listing each job's responsibility.

Blast radius: ci.yml only (+~50 lines refactor, -~15 lines from the existing checks mega-job). Zero source-logic changes. No new dependencies.

Prerequisites: None. Can land independently.

**Related:**
- br-0l5r (cross-platform matrix) — integrates into the split test job.
- br-kmbc (feature matrix) — integrates into the split test job.
- br-36qc (cargo-audit) + br-hg60 (secret scan) — natural new jobs alongside the split structure.
- br-zjme (fuzz CI — already landed with its own job) — this bead generalizes that pattern.
- br-0a83 (llvm-cov dead step) — cleaned up during the refactor.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T21:18:08.580789956Z","created_by":"cc-pandas","updated_at":"2026-04-22T21:18:08.580789956Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["ci","parallelism","review","triage"]}
